summary.overall: Chapter 1 was reviewed in 40 bounded source-grounded shards. Proposed changes: 64 replacements, 0 splits, and 7 removals.
patterns: Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect, appearing in all seven questions and all seven activities through stems or explanations.
Thin explanations that cite a paragraph number instead of teaching an operational principle are universal across questions and activities.
Paragraph 1-1-10 is overstuffed with four questions on a short source block — two multiple-choice and two true/false that retest the same facts.
Two knowledge-check activities (1-1-3 and 1-1-4) have correct answers that are substantially longer than their distractors, creating an answer-length cue.
Flashcards are uniformly well-formed: fronts are self-contained, backs are concise, no reverse-card or context problems.

Paragraph-location scaffolding: 14 of 21 items embed 'Under 1-1-X' in stems or 'Per 1-1-X' in explanations. This teaches paragraph-number recall instead of operational substance and is the dominant defect in this shard.

Thin explanations: Several items (especially true/false questions) use explanations that merely cite the paragraph location (e.g., 'That statement appears in 1-1-7c') instead of restating and teaching the operational requirement.

Flashcard duplication: Two flashcards for paragraph 1-1-6 test the cutoff-date definition with nearly identical wording — one referencing 'the publication table' and the other 'Table 1-1-1'. The concept is already covered adequately by the first card.

Table-lookup misalignment: Activity 63d1430c asks 'which source table identifies the appropriate headquarters contact path' rather than requiring the student to actually look up a specific contact from TBL 1-1-2. The question tests table-name recall, not the lookup skill the activity type implies.

Document-structure trivia: Flashcard 8c3f38b5 asks 'Where are significant changes to this order identified?' — a document-navigation question rather than one testing the retention advice that is the paragraph's substantive operational point.

generation_guidance: Strip all paragraph-number references from stems and explanations; a self-contained stem never needs a citation.
Write explanations that state the operational rule or obligation, not that a paragraph says something.
When a correct answer is longer because the source contains a compound statement, ensure distractors match in complexity so length alone does not signal the answer.
Limit each short source paragraph to one or two retrieval items; do not flood a paragraph with multiple question formats that retest the same facts.
True/false items should test a common operational misconception or a specific regulatory condition, not simply recite a source sentence.
Strip all 'Under 1-1-X' prefixes from question stems; the source paragraph is metadata for the authoring system, not part of the learning item. Operational questions should stand alone.
Strip all 'Per 1-1-X' citations from explanations. Explanations must teach the requirement, condition, or procedure directly from the source blocks — not merely cite a paragraph number.

For true/false items, write explanations that restate the controlling rule rather than pointing to a paragraph location. The explanation is the primary teaching opportunity when a student gets the answer wrong.

When creating table-lookup activities, ensure the question requires the student to find a specific value, row, or cell in the table — not merely to name which table applies. The activity type should match the cognitive skill.

Audit flashcard sets for near-duplicates: two cards that test the same concept in the same retrieval mode waste study time and risk confusion. When a concept is well-covered by one card, remove the weaker duplicate.

Prefer flashcard fronts and backs that test operational substance (retention advice, notification paths, submission requirements) over document-structure trivia (where in the order something appears, which table number covers a topic).
For procedural paragraphs like 1-1-8, ensure the submission chain is tested as a sequence (personnel → facility management → formal process) rather than as isolated facts, where format-appropriate.

Strip all paragraph-number references from stems and explanations. A self-contained stem never needs a citation like 'Under 1-1-9' or 'As used in 1-2-1.' The learner needs to know the rule, not where it lives in the document.

Write explanations that teach the controlling rule, condition, or operational distinction. Never reduce an explanation to 'Per X-X-X, [restate answer].' If a question asks about the routing chain for interpretation requests, explain why different requestor categories have different chains, not just which subparagraph lists them.

When a correct answer describes a multi-step procedural chain and each distractor is a single short phrase, the length disparity reveals the answer regardless of stored order. Match distractor detail to correct-answer detail.

Always include an explanation field in activity content objects. Different activity types carry different content schemas, but every activity benefits from an explanation that connects the answer to the operational principle.
Match item count to source substance. A single-sentence paragraph like 1-2-3 needs at most two retrieval items — one for recognition and one for recall — not five.

For definitional material like word meanings (1-2-1), a match-pairs activity is the right format, but it still needs an explanation that teaches the operational significance of the distinction (e.g., why 'will' meaning futurity vs. obligation matters when reading procedures).

When the source explicitly defines a term or procedure, the explanation may restate the definition directly. Do not add unsupported claims about safety outcomes, system design intent, or policy purpose that the source blocks do not themselves assert.

Strip all paragraph-number references from stems and explanations. A self-contained stem never needs 'Under 1-2-5' or 'As used in 1-2-4.' The learner needs to know what an annotation means, not which subparagraph letter it occupies.

Write explanations that teach the controlling rule rather than citing paragraph locations. If the source is a single-sentence definition like 1-2-4, the explanation may restate the definition with its source examples, but must not reduce to 'Per 1-2-4, [restate answer].'

Every activity content object must include an explanation field even when the activity type (match_pairs, sequence_steps) does not use the standard question-explanation-choices template. The match_pairs activity in this shard had no explanation at all.

For thin source material like document-convention paragraphs, keep item count proportionate. Paragraph 1-2-4's single sentence carries two activities and a flashcard — reasonable for reinforcement. Avoid exceeding one recognition item and one recall item per discrete concept when the source is definitional.

When an explanation teaches an exception or caveat (e.g., when paragraphs are NOT facility-annotated they apply to all facilities), include the full source statement in source_basis so the validator does not flag the explanation as adding unsupported content.
Avoid using 'this order' as the only context in stems that previously relied on paragraph citations. Replace with 'FAA JO 7110.65' or equivalent for clarity without location scaffolding.
Do not embed table or paragraph numbers in item stems, fronts, or explanations. Ask about the abbreviation substance directly.

For abbreviation-table items, accept that explanations will be thin — the source provides only mappings. Do not invent operational uses, safety rationales, or coordination contexts that are absent from the source blocks.
Definition flashcards for single-meaning abbreviations are format-correct with the expansion as the back. Do not inflate these with invented context.

When an abbreviation has multiple listed meanings (e.g., AAR, CFR), consider a concept card teaching contextual disambiguation as a complement to definition cards. The concept card in this shard (c3b804d9) is an effective example, once stripped of its table-number reference.
table_lookup activities should identify the source generically (e.g., 'the abbreviation table') rather than by its printed designation, unless the designation itself is the lookup target.
Paragraph 1-2-6 is a reference table — abbreviation flashcards are the most natural format. Future generation passes should continue producing abbreviation-definition cards for this paragraph.
The 'thin_back' heuristic should be suppressed for abbreviation-definition card types, or tuned to exclude definition cards whose source is a tabular abbreviation/glossary entry.

Consider supplementing abbreviation recall with recognition-format activities (e.g., multiple-choice questions asking which abbreviation corresponds to a given operational concept) to add retrieval diversity without inventing content absent from the source.

AM and AMB are long but source-accurate; splitting them into 'what does the abbreviation stand for' plus a separate 'which system (MEARTS or STARS)' card would create items with no source basis for the second card.
This shard covers only abbreviations starting with A–ANG from the full table; ensure the remaining alphabet is covered across other shards.
Do not add unsourced operational context (e.g., when or why an abbreviation is used in radio communication) — the source provides only the abbreviation and its expansion.

For abbreviation-table paragraphs, definition flashcards are the correct and sufficient format. Continue generating one card per abbreviation, matching the source expansion verbatim including parenthetical notes and punctuation.
Do not thicken abbreviation cards with invented operational context — the expansion IS the retrieval target and the source block provides no supporting rationale to draw from.

Consider supplementing abbreviation flashcards with a table-lookup activity that asks students to find the correct abbreviation or expansion from TBL 1-2-1 rather than recall it from memory, diversifying retrieval mode while respecting the reference-table nature of the source.
For future passes covering adjacent abbreviation ranges, maintain consistent card structure and verify every expansion against the source table character-by-character.
Continue generating definition flashcards for TBL 1-2-1 entries; the format is correct for this content type.
Do not suppress thin_back flags through artificial expansion — abbreviation strings that are genuinely short in the source should remain short.
Ensure full coverage of TBL 1-2-1 across all shards; this shard covers only a fraction (AAO through CEP) of the total abbreviation table.

Consider supplementing abbreviation flashcards with operational-context questions or activities in other paragraphs where the abbreviation is used in a controlling rule, so students learn the abbreviation in context rather than solely by rote.
For pure reference-table paragraphs like 1-2-6, definition-card drill is the appropriate ceiling. Do not force operational scenarios or decision questions where the source provides none.

Sample abbreviations across the full table range rather than clustering in one alphabet band. This shard's C–D concentration is a reasonable per-shard scope but the full chapter should distribute coverage.
Flag thin_back as informational for abbreviation cards rather than as a defect heuristic; a single-word or short-phrase back is the expected content for an abbreviation expansion.
Consider supplementing abbreviation cards with a 'source-use' activity that asks the student to locate a specific abbreviation in TBL 1-2-1, if paragraph coverage across the full chapter warrants it.

Paragraph 1-2-6 is a large reference table. Definition flashcards are the natural first format, but later generation passes could consider pairing abbreviation cards with context-use activities (e.g. a flight-strip scenario asking the user to interpret an abbreviation in context) or multiple-choice questions that test recognition of the correct expansion among plausible distractors.

Abbreviation-range shards will routinely produce no-intervention manifests when the source is a table and the cards are simple definition expansions. The orchestrator should not interpret empty decisions arrays as incomplete review.

shard_overviews: This shard covers introductory material (purpose, audience, LOAs, waivers, SMS, references, distribution) that is straightforward but administrative. Items are generally source-accurate. The primary defect is paragraph-location scaffolding: nearly every question stem and explanation embeds a paragraph-number reference that substitutes document trivia for operational knowledge. The four LOA items in paragraph 1-1-10 are the most problematic, with three carrying major-severity scaffolding and one true/false item reduced to an explanation that merely states the source paragraph number. Two knowledge-check activities exhibit answer-length cues where the single correct answer is far longer than the distractors. Flashcards are uniformly clean with no defects. No items required removal or splitting.

This shard covers four administrative paragraphs (1-1-5 through 1-1-8) dealing with document handling, publication schedules, delivery procedures, and change submission processes. The underlying content is source-faithful with correct answers throughout — no factual errors were found. However, the items suffer from two pervasive quality defects: paragraph-location scaffolding in nearly every stem and explanation, and thin or citation-only explanations that fail to teach the operational requirement. One flashcard is a near-duplicate of another testing the same concept in the same retrieval mode. One table-lookup activity asks students to name a table rather than use it, misaligning the activity type with the task. One flashcard teaches document-structure trivia (where changes appear) rather than the substantive retention advice from the same paragraph. These are all correctable quality issues; the content foundation is sound.

This shard covers four brief administrative and definitional paragraphs (1-1-9, 1-2-1, 1-2-2, 1-2-3). Items are source-accurate — no factual errors were found. The dominant defect is pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: nearly every question stem and explanation embeds a paragraph-number citation that substitutes document navigation trivia for operational knowledge. Explanations in questions and activities are generally thin, often restating the source sentence with a 'Per X-X-X' prefix instead of teaching the controlling principle. One activity (match_pairs for word meanings) has no explanation at all. One question (c63e034b) has a correct answer several times longer than any distractor, creating an answer-length cue. Paragraph 1-2-3 is a single sentence yet carries three activity items plus a question and a flashcard — excessive coverage that dilutes retrieval diversity. Flashcards are uniformly clean with no defects. Two activities under 1-2-3 are removed as redundant. Sixteen items are replaced to strip scaffolding, improve explanations, or balance answer choices.

This shard covers two thin administrative paragraphs (1-2-4 References and 1-2-5 Annotations) that define document conventions rather than air traffic control procedures. All nine items are source-accurate with no factual errors. The dominant defect is paragraph-location scaffolding across questions and explanations: stems begin with 'Under 1-2-5' and explanations use 'Per 1-2-5c' or similar citations instead of teaching the annotation's operational meaning. One true/false question (b02e2a18) has an explanation that is literally 'That statement appears in 1-2-5d' — a citation with zero educational content. Two activity explanations for 1-2-4 are thin paragraph-citation restatements, and the match_pairs activity for 1-2-5 has no explanation field at all. Both flashcards are clean, concise, and self-contained. All seven items with defects are replaced; no removals or splits are needed because no item is factually wrong, redundant, or overloaded.

operations.source_basis: As used in this order: a. 'Must' means a procedure is mandatory.

match.question_text: As used in 1-2-1, what does the word "must" mean?

replacements.question_text: When this order uses the word 'must,' what does it indicate about the associated procedure?
replacements.explanation: In this order, 'must' means the procedure is mandatory — compliance is required, not optional or recommended. This is the strongest affirmative obligation the order can place on a controller. Distinguishing 'must' from 'should' (recommended), 'may' (optional), and 'will' (futurity only) is fundamental to interpreting every other provision of the order correctly.

choices.text: The procedure is mandatory.
The procedure is recommended.
The procedure is optional.
A future action is anticipated but not required.

operations.problem: The stem begins with 'As used in 1-2-1' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation cites 'Per 1-2-1e' instead of teaching why the futurity meaning matters operationally. Understanding that 'will' does not create an obligation is critical for reading ATC procedures correctly, but the current explanation does not convey this.
operations.source_basis: As used in this order: e. 'Will' means futurity, not a requirement for the application of a procedure.

match.question_text: As used in 1-2-1, what does the word "will" mean?

replacements.question_text: When this order uses the word 'will,' what does it indicate about the associated procedure?
replacements.explanation: In this order, 'will' expresses futurity only — it describes something that is going to happen, not something that a controller is required to make happen. This is a critical distinction: 'will' carries no obligation. A controller who misreads 'will' as 'must' could incorrectly treat a descriptive statement as a mandatory procedure. Only 'must' creates a binding requirement in this order.

choices.text: Futurity — describes what is going to happen, not a requirement to apply a procedure.
The procedure is mandatory.
The procedure is prohibited.
A controller may choose whether to comply.

operations.source_basis: As used in this order: a. 'Must' means a procedure is mandatory. b. 'Must not' means a procedure is prohibited. c. 'Should' means a procedure is recommended. d. 'May' or 'need not' means a procedure is optional. e. 'Will' means futurity, not a requirement for the application of a procedure.

content.instruction: Match each term with its meaning in this order.

pairs.definition: futurity, not a requirement for the application of a procedure

content.instruction: Match each term with its meaning in this order.
content.explanation: The order uses specific words to signal the force of each procedure. 'Must' and 'must not' create binding obligations and prohibitions — compliance is not discretionary. 'Should' signals a recommendation that may be deviated from with reason. 'May' and 'need not' leave the action to the controller's discretion. 'Will' expresses futurity only: it describes what is going to happen, not what is required to happen. Correctly distinguishing these five terms is foundational to interpreting every other paragraph in the order.

pairs.definition: futurity, not a requirement for the application of a procedure

content.question_text: What concept does the divergence figure help the learner interpret?

choices.text: The angular divergence concept used when distinguishing course relationships.
A required radio phraseology format for divergent departures.
A wake turbulence category table.
A list of military distribution contacts.

content.explanation: The divergence figure is a geometry aid that illustrates angular divergence between courses. It supports the course definitions in this order: same courses (less than 45 degrees angular difference), crossing courses (45 through 135 degrees inclusive), and opposite or reciprocal courses (greater than 135 through 180 degrees inclusive). The figure helps visualize the protected airspace relationships that determine which separation criteria apply.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under 1-2-2' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation reduces to 'Per 1-2-2b, these are crossing courses' — a thin citation that adds nothing beyond the answer. The question tests course classification by angle band, which is core operational knowledge, but the presentation frames it as paragraph trivia.
operations.source_basis: CROSSING COURSES are intersecting courses whose angular difference is 45 through 135 degrees inclusive.

match.question_text: Under 1-2-2, how are intersecting courses with an angular difference of 45 through 135 degrees inclusive classified?

replacements.question_text: How are intersecting courses classified when their angular difference is 45 through 135 degrees inclusive?
replacements.explanation: The order divides course relationships into three angle bands. Crossing courses occupy the middle band: 45 through 135 degrees inclusive. Same courses are the narrowest band (less than 45 degrees), and opposite or reciprocal courses are the widest (greater than 135 through 180 degrees inclusive). The classification determines which separation criteria must be applied.

choices.text: Crossing courses.
Same courses.
Opposite or reciprocal courses.
Diverging protected courses.

operations.problem: The stem opens with 'What does the note in 1-2-2 say about...' (paragraph-location scaffolding). The explanation uses 'Per the note in 1-2-2' as a prefix. The question tests the relationship between protected airspace overlap and lateral separation — an operationally important concept — but frames it as a note-location exercise.
operations.source_basis: The term 'protected airspace,' as used in this paragraph, is the airspace equal to one half the required applicable lateral separation on either side of an aircraft along its projected flight path. If the protected airspace of two aircraft does not overlap, applicable lateral separation is ensured.

match.question_text: What does the note in 1-2-2 say about protected airspace and lateral separation?

replacements.question_text: What is the relationship between protected airspace overlap and lateral separation under the course definitions in this order?
replacements.explanation: Protected airspace is defined as the airspace equal to one half the required applicable lateral separation on either side of an aircraft along its projected flight path. The key operational principle is: when the protected airspace of two aircraft does not overlap, applicable lateral separation is ensured. When protected airspaces do overlap, the course relationship (same, crossing, or opposite/reciprocal) determines which separation minima apply.

choices.text: If the protected airspace of two aircraft does not overlap, applicable lateral separation is ensured.
Protected airspace is one quarter of the applicable lateral separation on each side of the aircraft's projected flight path.
Protected airspace applies only to opposite-course operations.
Lateral separation exists only when the projected flight paths are exactly parallel.

replacements.explanation: The angle range 45 through 135 degrees inclusive defines crossing courses, not opposite or reciprocal courses. Opposite or reciprocal courses occupy the widest angle band: greater than 135 through 180 degrees inclusive. The three course classifications are: same courses (less than 45 degrees), crossing courses (45 through 135 degrees inclusive), and opposite or reciprocal courses (greater than 135 through 180 degrees inclusive).

replacements.explanation: Notes in this order serve a supporting role: they present statements of fact or material of a prefatory or explanatory nature that relates to the directive text. operations.source_basis: Bold vertical lines in the margin of changed pages indicate the location of substantive revisions to the order. Bold vertical lines adjacent to the title of a chapter, section, or paragraph means that extensive changes have been made to that chapter, section, or paragraph.

match.question_text: Under 1-2-5, what do bold vertical lines in the margin of changed pages indicate?

replacements.question_text: In FAA JO 7110.65, what do bold vertical lines in the margin of changed pages indicate?
replacements.explanation: Bold vertical lines in the margin of changed pages indicate the location of substantive revisions to the order. When bold vertical lines appear adjacent to the title of a chapter, section, or paragraph, they mean that extensive changes have been made to that chapter, section, or paragraph. The distinction between margin lines (substantive changes within a page) and title-adjacent lines (extensive changes to an entire section) helps the reader assess the scope of revisions at a glance.

Paragraphs that apply only to military facilities.

Controllers are expected, to the extent practical, to approximate the words and/or phrases used in the example. operations.problem: The match_pairs activity content object has no explanation field at all. The four annotation pairs (PHRASEOLOGY, EXAMPLE, WAKE TURBULENCE APPLICATION, EN ROUTE/OCEANIC/TERMINAL) are accurately matched to their source definitions, and the match-pairs format is well-suited to this annotation-to-meaning retrieval task. However, without an explanation, the activity provides no operational context for why these annotations matter when reading the order — the student memorizes pairings without understanding the significance of each annotation type.
operations.source_basis: The annotation PHRASEOLOGY denotes prescribed words and/or phrases. The annotation EXAMPLE provides a sample of the way prescribed phraseology associated with the preceding paragraph(s) will be used. WAKE TURBULENCE APPLICATION inserted within a paragraph means that the remaining information in the paragraph requires the application of wake turbulence procedures. Paragraphs annotated with EN ROUTE, OCEANIC, or TERMINAL are only to be applied by the designated type facility.

content.instruction: Match each annotation to what it tells you.

pairs.definition: prescribed words or phrases to be used in communications
sample of the way associated phraseology will be used
the remaining information in the paragraph requires wake turbulence procedures
the paragraph applies only to the designated type of facility

content.instruction: Match each annotation to what it tells you.
content.explanation: Each annotation in FAA JO 7110.65 carries a specific operational meaning. PHRASEOLOGY marks prescribed words and phrases to be used in communications — these are mandatory, though controllers may rephrase afterward for clarity. EXAMPLE provides a sample of how the associated phraseology will be used; when no prescribed phraseology precedes it, the example text is not mandatory but should be approximated. WAKE TURBULENCE APPLICATION means all remaining information in that paragraph requires wake turbulence procedures. EN ROUTE, OCEANIC, or TERMINAL means the paragraph applies only to the designated facility type; paragraphs without this annotation apply to all facilities.
